Q1: Honestly, my study routine was kind of scattered even though I put in about 25 hours a week. I'm a visual learner, so I'd block out time to watch lecture videos and create diagrams or mind maps, but I'd often get bored halfway through and switch to something else. I tried to keep a schedule, but my motivation just wasn't there most of the time, so I'd end up cramming more than I'd like to admit. My extracurriculars also ate into my planned study time pretty regularly.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and how anxious I was feeling about upcoming assignments. Early in the semester I'd try to study at consistent times, but that fell apart quickly when other responsibilities piled up. Toward deadline weeks, I'd suddenly be online constantly, then barely touch anything for days after. The inconsistency stressed me out, but I couldn't seem to break the cycle.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or pure guilt that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd see a notification about a discussion post or a new module and that would prompt me. Other times I'd just be bored and figure I should at least check what was due soon. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me logging in more often because there was something slightly more interesting to engage with.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine with coffee. I'd scroll through announcements and see if there were any new discussion posts to respond to. But honestly, it felt more like a habit than genuine engagement—I was going through the motions. When the gamified elements were added, it became slightly more appealing, but even that wore off after a few weeks.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the visual content at my own pace. I also found the discussion forums helpful when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of. The gamified tasks were actually decent for keeping me engaged when I was feeling bored, which was often. Anything with visual diagrams or interactive elements worked better for my learning style.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the supplementary reading materials—they were just walls of text and I couldn't stay focused on them. The optional quizzes I'd delay until the last possible moment or skip entirely. Some of the peer review activities felt tedious, so I'd do the bare minimum. If something wasn't visually engaging or didn't have clear immediate relevance, it got pushed to the bottom of my priority list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to my assignments or exams. If it was interactive or had some visual component, that helped a lot too. The gamified tasks felt more worthwhile because there was immediate feedback and a sense of progress. Honestly, if something was just busywork with no clear payoff, I couldn't bring myself to care about it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they existed in a different category entirely—like nice-to-haves that I'd get to 'someday' but never did. Required activities carried this weight of anxiety because I knew they'd affect my grade. With optional stuff, there was no consequence for skipping them, so when I was bored or stressed, they were the first to go. The gamified optional tasks were slightly more appealing, but still not as urgent.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and videos to my laptop and study from there without being connected to the LMS. I used a lot of external YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't fully grasp from the course materials. I also had a study group through WhatsApp where we'd share notes and explain things to each other. A lot of my actual learning happened in those spaces that the system couldn't track.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or working through problems on paper that I'd copied from the course materials. I'd also spend hours creating visual study aids like color-coded charts that helped me understand relationships between concepts. Sometimes I'd be discussing course content with friends over coffee without any digital trace. The LMS only saw the tip of the iceberg of my actual study time.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download the accompanying slides, and then review those slides multiple times offline. Same with PDFs—I'd open them once to download, then study from my laptop's local files. It was just more convenient than logging in every time, especially when my internet was being slow or I wanted to study somewhere without WiFi.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. I was juggling extracurriculars, my anxiety was through the roof, and I just felt completely drained. There were weeks where I'd stare at the LMS homepage and feel this overwhelming sense of boredom mixed with dread. When the workload from multiple courses peaked simultaneously, this course often got deprioritized because I felt like I could 'catch up later.'
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from other responsibilities, I'd log into the LMS but just mindlessly scroll without actually processing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely rather than face them. Competing deadlines from my extracurriculars meant the LMS sometimes went untouched for days. My medium stress level was basically constant, which made sustained engagement really difficult.
Q14: Oh, all the time. I'd have videos playing while I was actually scrolling through my phone or thinking about something else entirely. Sometimes I'd click through modules just to mark them as 'viewed' without really absorbing the content. There were discussion posts I'd skim and respond to superficially just to meet participation requirements. My mind was elsewhere even when my activity logs looked productive.
Q15: I postponed the longer written assignments constantly, even though I knew they were heavily weighted. The anxiety of starting something big made me avoid it until the pressure became unbearable. I'd tell myself I needed to 'be in the right headspace' to do quality work, but that headspace rarely came. Sometimes I'd do easier, less important tasks just to feel productive while avoiding the hard stuff.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd have periods of almost no activity followed by frantic bursts right before something was due. The deadline pressure was the main thing that could cut through my boredom and low motivation. I'd set my own fake earlier deadlines sometimes, but I never actually believed them. My response time averaging almost 23 hours reflects that last-minute scramble pattern.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got positive feedback, it gave me a temporary boost, but it wore off quickly. Critical feedback sometimes made me anxious and avoidant of that type of task in the future. The gamified elements provided more immediate feedback which I found more engaging than waiting days for instructor comments. Overall, feedback mattered, but it didn't fundamentally change my engagement patterns.
Q18: The course structure with its weekly modules gave me a framework, but it also made things feel repetitive and predictable, which fed into my boredom. Having clear deadlines helped me prioritize, but the sheer number of online courses I was taking made it hard to keep up with all of them. The gamified intervention actually helped break up the monotony somewhat. I wished there was more flexibility in how I could demonstrate my learning.
Q19: The visual resources and video content made engagement easier for someone like me. However, the heavy text-based readings were a barrier—I'd zone out quickly. The discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, which made them feel like a chore rather than genuine learning. The gamified tasks were a nice addition that made things slightly more interesting. Clearer connections between activities and assessments would have helped my motivation.
Q20: I think that describes me sometimes, honestly. You can be clicking around, watching videos, participating in discussions, but still not producing finished work because of anxiety or perfectionism. Sometimes high activity is actually procrastination—staying busy with easier tasks to avoid the harder assignments. Or maybe someone understands the material but struggles with the specific format of the required submissions. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That resonates with me too. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show up in the logs—studying downloaded materials, working with my study group, creating my own visual aids. Someone could have low LMS activity but be genuinely engaging with the content through other means. Also, some people are just efficient—they don't need to rewatch videos five times or participate in every discussion to understand the material.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active, how often you log in, and what resources you click on. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement—whether you're actually thinking or just going through the motions. They can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or whether you're genuinely learning versus just performing engagement. The numbers tell a story, but it's a very incomplete one.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling mentally present when you're studying. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's when the content genuinely interests you and you want to understand it deeply. For me, real engagement is rare because of my low motivation and frequent boredom, but when it happens, it's when I'm creating something visual or discussing ideas with peers. It's more about mental investment than time spent.
Q24: The LMS can't show my constant battle with anxiety and boredom, or how hard I have to push myself just to open the course page some days. It doesn't capture the quality conversations I have with my study group or the detailed visual notes I create offline. It can't measure my resilience—how I keep showing up despite feeling unmotivated and stressed. And it definitely can't show how the gamified tasks briefly sparked something in me before the novelty wore off. My engagement is complicated, and the data only scratches the surface.
